step2_rag_chunks: Patients who smoke their first cigarette within 30 minutes of waking up, or who smoke more than 15 cigarettes per day, often have strong physical nicotine dependence. Patient: I work from home and since 2020 my smoking has more than doubled. I used to smoke 5 a day and now it's 18-20.

Patient: It's like I have no schedule and my brain gets really bored. I keep switching between tasks and that leads to procrastination, and I find myself smoking more during those times.

step2_rag_chunks: Keeping your hands busy with activities such as drawing, writing, or using a stress ball can help reduce the urge to smoke.

After meals, immediately introduce a competing behavior before the urge has a chance to form â€” get up from the table, brush your teeth, chew sugar-free gum, or take a short walk. The goal is to break the automatic link between finishing a meal and reaching for a cigarette by making a different behavior equally automatic over time.
Replacing smoking with another activity such as chewing gum, eating healthy snacks, or walking can help break the habit.

step2_rag_chunks: Cravings for tobacco are temporary and usually last between 5 and 10 minutes. When a craving occurs, delay smoking and focus on another activity until the urge passes.

When a craving hits, delaying the response by even 5 to 10 minutes is often enough for it to pass. Tell yourself you will smoke in 10 minutes if you still want to â€” and then do something else for those 10 minutes. Most of the time the urge has weakened significantly by the time the 10 minutes are up.

Patients who smoke their first cigarette within 30 minutes of waking up, or who smoke more than 15 cigarettes per day, often have strong physical nicotine dependence. For these patients, combining behavioral strategies with nicotine replacement therapy gives better results than behavioral strategies alone.
